title: Dyeing with birch leaves
subtitle: A warm yellow to golden tan dye from fresh or dried birch leaves on alum-mordanted wool
excerpt: Birch leaves are an accessible foraged dye plant, producing clear yellow to warm gold on alum-mordanted wool. Leaves gathered in summer give the strongest colour; autumn leaves dye paler. This entry covers mordanting, preparing the dye bath from fresh or dried leaves, and dyeing a 100 g skein.
sourceNotes: Birch leaf dyeing synthesised from general natural dyeing references. Birch (Betula pendula and related species) is a well-documented dye plant producing flavonoid yellows on alum-mordanted protein fibres.

glossaryTerms.definition: A mineral salt, usually potassium alum, applied to the fibre before dyeing to help the dye bond permanently to the fibre. Without a mordant, most natural dyes wash out quickly.
The dry weight of the fibre being dyed, used as the reference for calculating mordant and dye plant quantities. Abbreviated WOF.
The liquid obtained by simmering the dye plant material in water. The fibre is added to the strained dye bath to take up colour.
When a dye bath has given up most of its available colour to the fibre and subsequent skeins dye progressively lighter. An exhausted dye bath can be discarded or used to dye pale tones.

attrs.title: Foraging note
attrs.body: Only harvest birch leaves where you have permission. Take no more than one-third of the leaves from any single branch.

content.text: Birch leaves contain flavonoid dyes that bond well to alum-mordanted wool, producing a clear, sunny yellow when gathered in summer and a softer gold from autumn leaves. The colour is reasonably lightfast for a flavonoid yellow. The
dye bath
is gentle and easy to manage: birch does not require strong acids or long simmering. A 200%
weight of fibre
of fresh leaves (or 100% of dried leaves) gives a good depth of colour.

items.name: Cream of tartar (optional, brightens colour), 6% WOF
Stainless steel pot, at least 6 litre capacity

content.text: Method
Step 1: Mordant the fibre

Dissolve the potassium alum and cream of tartar in a small amount of hot water, then add to the pot with enough water to cover the fibre. Add the pre-wetted wool skein. Heat slowly to 85°C over 30 minutes, stirring gently. Hold at 85°C for 45 minutes. Lift the skein, allow to cool in the
mordant
liquid, then squeeze gently. The mordanted fibre can be used immediately or wrapped in a damp cloth and refrigerated for up to three days.
Step 2: Prepare the dye bath

Pack the fresh or dried birch leaves into the pot with enough water to cover generously. Bring to the boil over medium heat, then simmer for 45 minutes. The water will turn a clear amber-yellow. Strain the plant material out through a sieve or cloth and return the liquid to the pot. If the colour looks pale, the leaves may have been gathered late in the season or dried too long: add a second batch of fresh leaves and re-simmer.
Step 3: Dye the fibre

Add the mordanted fibre to the strained dye bath. Heat slowly to 80-85°C over 20 minutes, stirring gently to ensure even colour. Hold at temperature for 45 minutes without boiling (boiling stresses the fibre without improving the dye uptake). Let the skein cool in the bath to deepen the colour. When the
dye bath
is nearly clear and the fibre has reached the desired shade, lift the skein and rinse in water of the same temperature.
Step 4: Rinse and dry

Rinse the skein in two changes of warm water until the water runs clear. Squeeze gently without wringing. Hang to dry away from direct sunlight: strong UV light fades flavonoid yellows more quickly than indirect light. Once dry, the colour will be slightly lighter than the wet skein. The
exhaust
bath can be used immediately to dye a second skein in a paler gold.

items.problem: The colour is pale and washed-out after dyeing
items.cause: The mordant was not applied correctly, or the leaves were gathered too late in the season
items.fix: Check the mordant step: alum must fully dissolve before the fibre is added, and the temperature must reach and hold at 85°C. Summer leaves give the strongest colour; try gathering earlier in the season.

items.problem: The colour fades noticeably after washing
items.cause: The mordanting step was rushed or the alum percentage was too low
items.fix: Re-mordant the skein with a fresh 15% alum bath, then overdye in a fresh birch leaf bath. Flavonoid yellows need a good mordant bond to be washfast.

items.problem: The fibre has felted slightly during dyeing
items.cause: The temperature was too high or the pot was stirred too vigorously
items.fix: Keep the temperature at or below 85°C throughout. Stir gently and infrequently, and lower the temperature if the water surface begins to break into a rolling simmer.
